data.content:
### Define Dynamic Matrix from Job Output in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

Use job outputs to dynamically define matrix values for dependent jobs. This workflow demonstrates a three-job pattern: the first job outputs a JSON array, the second job uses that array to generate artifacts, and the third job consumes those artifacts. The `fromJSON()` function converts the string output into a usable matrix.

```yaml
name: shared matrix
on:
  push:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  define-matrix:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    outputs:
      colors: ${{ steps.colors.outputs.colors }}

    steps:
      - name: Define Colors
        id: colors
        run: |
          echo 'colors=["red", "green", "blue"]' >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

  produce-artifacts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: define-matrix
    strategy:
      matrix:
        color: ${{ fromJSON(needs.define-matrix.outputs.colors) }}

    steps:
      - name: Define Color
        env:
          color: ${{ matrix.color }}
        run: |
          echo "$color" > color
      - name: Produce Artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: ${{ matrix.color }}
          path: color

  consume-artifacts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs:
    - define-matrix
    - produce-artifacts
    strategy:
      matrix:
        color: ${{ fromJSON(needs.define-matrix.outputs.colors) }}

    steps:
    - name: Retrieve Artifact
      uses: actions/download-artifact@v5
      with:
        name: ${{ matrix.color }}

    - name: Report Color
      run: |
        cat color
```

--------------------------------

### GitHub Action with Matrix Strategy for Unique Artifacts

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/contexts

This GitHub Actions workflow demonstrates how to use the `strategy` context, specifically `strategy.job-index`, to create unique log files and artifact names for each job in a matrix. The workflow uses a matrix with `test-group` and `node` variables. It outputs a mock log file and uploads it as an artifact, ensuring distinct artifacts for each matrix combination.

```yaml
name: Test strategy
on: push

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        test-group: [1, 2]
        node: [14, 16]
    steps:
      - run: echo "Mock test logs" > test-job-${{ strategy.job-index }}.txt
      - name: Upload logs
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: Build log for job ${{ strategy.job-index }}
          path: test-job-${{ strategy.job-index }}.txt
```

--------------------------------

### Exclude Matrix Configurations in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

Use the `exclude` property to remove specific configurations from a matrix strategy. This reduces the total number of jobs that will run by filtering out unwanted combinations of matrix variables. The example excludes one macOS job and two Windows jobs from a 12-job matrix, resulting in 9 total jobs.

```yaml
strategy:
  matrix:
    os: [macos-latest, windows-latest]
    version: [12, 14, 16]
    environment: [staging, production]
    exclude:
      - os: macos-latest
        version: 12
        environment: production
      - os: windows-latest
        version: 16
runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
```

--------------------------------

### Collect and Access Matrix Job Outputs in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-your-workflow-with-github-actions/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This snippet illustrates how outputs from multiple matrix jobs are aggregated and accessed by a dependent job. It defines outputs that vary based on the matrix strategy and then shows how a downstream job can retrieve the combined outputs as a JSON object.

```yaml
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      output_1: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_1 }}
      output_2: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_2 }}
      output_3: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_3 }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: [1, 2, 3]
    steps:
      - name: Generate output
        id: gen_output
        run: |
          version="${{ matrix.version }}"
          echo "output_${version}=${version}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
  job2:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [job1]
    steps:
      # Will show
      # {
      #   "output_1": "1",
      #   "output_2": "2",
      #   "output_3": "3"
      # }
      - run: echo '${{ toJSON(needs.job1.outputs) }}'
```

--------------------------------

### Illustrate GitHub Actions Matrix Job Combinations from Object Arrays

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This YAML snippet shows the resulting job combinations generated by a matrix defined with an array of objects. It details how `os` and `node` properties, including nested `env` variables, are combined for each individual job created by the matrix strategy.

```yaml
- matrix.os: ubuntu-latest
  matrix.node.version: 14
- matrix.os: ubuntu-latest
  matrix.node.version: 20
  matrix.node.env: NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider
- matrix.os: macos-latest
  matrix.node.version: 14
- matrix.os: macos-latest
  matrix.node.version: 20
  matrix.node.env: NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider

```

--------------------------------

### Generate Multiple Outputs Using Matrix Strategy in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

Use matrix jobs to generate multiple outputs with different names. Job outputs from all matrix jobs are combined, with the last job run overwriting any duplicate output names. Ensure unique output names to prevent unintended overrides.

```yaml
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      output_1: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_1 }}
      output_2: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_2 }}
      output_3: ${{ steps.gen_output.outputs.output_3 }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: [1, 2, 3]
    steps:
      - name: Generate output
        id: gen_output
        run: |
          version="${{ matrix.version }}"
          echo "output_${version}=${version}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
  job2:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [job1]
    steps:
      - run: echo '${{ toJSON(needs.job1.outputs) }}'
```

--------------------------------

### jobs.<job_id>.strategy.matrix

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax

Defines the matrix of different job configurations within a strategy. Variables defined here become properties in the `matrix` context and generate up to 256 jobs per workflow run.

```APIDOC
## jobs.<job_id>.strategy.matrix

### Description
Use `jobs.<job_id>.strategy.matrix` to define a matrix of different job configurations. For more information, see Running variations of jobs in a workflow.
A matrix will generate a maximum of 256 jobs per workflow run. This limit applies to both GitHub-hosted and self-hosted runners.
The variables that you define become properties in the `matrix` context, and you can reference the property in other areas of your workflow file. In this example, you can use `matrix.version` and `matrix.os` to access the current value of `version` and `os` that the job is using.
By default, GitHub will maximize the number of jobs run in parallel depending on runner availability. The order of the variables in the matrix determines the order in which the jobs are created. The first variable you define will be the first job that is created in your workflow run.

### Type
Configuration

### Path
jobs.<job_id>.strategy.matrix

### Parameters
#### Configuration Fields
- **KEY** (array) - Required - A list of values for a specific matrix variable (e.g., `version`, `os`).

### Configuration Example
```yaml
jobs:
  example_matrix:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: [10, 12, 14]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.version }}

```
```

--------------------------------

### Define a Basic Matrix Strategy in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

This YAML code demonstrates how to define a basic matrix strategy within a GitHub Actions job. It creates a matrix with 'version' and 'os' variables, leading to multiple job runs for each combination. This is useful for testing across different environments and configurations.

```yaml
jobs:
  example_matrix:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: [10, 12, 14]
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]

```

--------------------------------

### Create Dynamic Matrix from Context in GitHub Actions Workflow

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

This YAML workflow demonstrates how to create a dynamic matrix using contexts, specifically from a 'repository_dispatch' event's payload. It defines a job that uses 'github.event.client_payload.versions' to set the 'version' matrix variable, enabling flexible testing based on event data rather than static definitions.

```yaml
on:
  repository_dispatch:
    types:
      - test

jobs:
  example_matrix:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: ${{ github.event.client_payload.versions }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.version }}

```

--------------------------------

### Limit Concurrent Jobs in Matrix Strategy GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

Use the `max-parallel` property to control the maximum number of jobs that run simultaneously in a matrix strategy. This example limits concurrent execution to 2 jobs, even though the matrix would normally generate 6 jobs (3 versions × 2 operating systems). Useful for managing resource constraints or API rate limits.

```yaml
jobs:
  example_matrix:
    strategy:
      max-parallel: 2
      matrix:
        version: [10, 12, 14]
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
```

--------------------------------

### Matrix Context Usage

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/accessing-contextual-information-about-workflow-runs

The matrix context allows you to create multiple job variations by defining multiple values for different parameters. This example demonstrates how to use the matrix context to run tests across different operating systems and Node.js versions.

```APIDOC
## Matrix Context

### Description
The `matrix` context enables you to create a matrix of jobs with different parameter combinations. Each combination creates a separate job instance with access to the specific matrix values.

### Context Properties
- **matrix.<key>** (string/number/boolean) - Required - Individual matrix variable values accessible within each job

### Usage in Workflow
Access matrix values using the syntax `${{ matrix.<key> }}`

### Request Example
```yaml
name: Test matrix
on: push

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        node: [14, 16]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node }}
      - name: Output node version
        run: node --version
```

### Parameters
#### Matrix Variables
- **os** (string) - Operating system for the job runner. Values: ubuntu-latest, windows-latest
- **node** (number) - Node.js version to use. Values: 14, 16

### Response
#### Job Execution
This configuration creates 4 separate job runs (2 operating systems × 2 Node.js versions), each with the corresponding matrix values injected into the workflow.
```

--------------------------------

### Example Event Payload for Dynamic GitHub Actions Matrix

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

This JSON snippet illustrates an example 'repository_dispatch' event payload. This payload can be used to dynamically populate matrix variables in a GitHub Actions workflow, allowing for flexible job configurations based on external triggers or custom inputs.

```json
{
  "event_type": "test",
  "client_payload": {
    "versions": [12, 14, 16]
  }
}

```

--------------------------------

### Allow Specific Matrix Jobs to Fail in GitHub Actions with continue-on-error

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This configuration shows how to use `continue-on-error` within a job matrix to prevent specific experimental jobs from failing the entire workflow run. By setting `fail-fast: false` and `continue-on-error` to true for designated matrix combinations, the workflow can proceed even if those jobs encounter errors.

```yaml
runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
continue-on-error: ${{ matrix.experimental }}
strategy:
  fail-fast: false
  matrix:
    node: [13, 14]
    os: [macos-latest, ubuntu-latest]
    experimental: [false]
    include:
      - node: 15
        os: ubuntu-latest
        experimental: true
```

--------------------------------

### Share Data Between Jobs Using Artifacts in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/store-and-share-data

A complete GitHub Actions workflow that demonstrates passing data between three dependent jobs using upload-artifact and download-artifact actions. Job 1 performs a calculation and uploads the result, Job 2 downloads and processes it, and Job 3 displays the final result. The workflow uses the needs keyword to ensure sequential execution across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS runners.

```yaml
name: Share data between jobs

on: [push]

jobs:
  job_1:
    name: Add 3 and 7
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - shell: bash
        run: |
          expr 3 + 7 > math-homework.txt
      - name: Upload math result for job 1
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: homework_pre
          path: math-homework.txt

  job_2:
    name: Multiply by 9
    needs: job_1
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
      - name: Download math result for job 1
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v5
        with:
          name: homework_pre
      - shell: bash
        run: |
          value=`cat math-homework.txt`
          expr $value \* 9 > math-homework.txt
      - name: Upload math result for job 2
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: homework_final
          path: math-homework.txt

  job_3:
    name: Display results
    needs: job_2
    runs-on: macOS-latest
    steps:
      - name: Download math result for job 2
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v5
        with:
          name: homework_final
      - name: Print the final result
        shell: bash
        run: |
          value=`cat math-homework.txt`
          echo The result is $value
```

--------------------------------

### Define GitHub Actions Jobs Solely with Matrix 'include'

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-your-workflow-with-github-actions/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This workflow shows how to leverage the 'include' property within a matrix strategy to define jobs when no base matrix variables are specified. Each entry in 'include' acts as a distinct job configuration. This approach is beneficial for defining a fixed set of parameterized jobs without the combinatorial explosion of a full matrix.

```yaml
jobs:
  includes_only:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        include:
          - site: "production"
            datacenter: "site-a"
          - site: "staging"
            datacenter: "site-b"
```

--------------------------------

### Expand or Add Matrix Configurations with 'include' in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

This YAML snippet shows how to expand or add new matrix configurations using the 'include' keyword within a GitHub Actions strategy. The 'include' list can add new variables to existing combinations or create entirely new job configurations, offering granular control over matrix generation.

```yaml
strategy:
  matrix:
    fruit: [apple, pear]
    animal: [cat, dog]
    include:
      - color: green
      - color: pink
        animal: cat
      - fruit: apple
        shape: circle
      - fruit: banana
      - fruit: banana
        animal: cat

```

--------------------------------

### Matrix Context Properties

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/contexts

Access custom matrix properties defined in the workflow file through the matrix context. Each matrix property becomes accessible as a context variable for the current job execution.

```APIDOC
## Matrix Context

### Description
The `matrix` context contains the matrix properties defined in the workflow file that apply to the current job. This context is only available for jobs in a matrix and changes for each job in a workflow run.

### Context Properties
- **matrix.<property_name>** (string) - The value of a custom matrix property defined in the workflow

### Custom Properties
No standard properties exist in the matrix context. Only properties explicitly defined in the workflow's matrix configuration are available.

### Example Context Value
```json
{
  "os": "ubuntu-latest",
  "node": 16
}
```

This example shows a matrix job executing with `os` set to `ubuntu-latest` and `node` set to `16`.

### Accessing Matrix Properties
- Reference matrix properties using `${{ matrix.property_name }}` syntax
- Each job in the matrix receives the corresponding property values
- Useful for parameterizing steps based on matrix combinations
- Common matrix properties include os, node-version, python-version, etc.
```

--------------------------------

### Matrix Context

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/contexts-reference

The matrix context contains the matrix properties defined in the workflow file that apply to the current job. It includes dynamic properties based on the matrix configuration.

```APIDOC
## Matrix Context

### Description
Access matrix properties defined in the workflow file for the current job.

### Context Properties
- **matrix** (object) - Contains all matrix properties defined in workflow
- **matrix.<property_name>** (string) - The value of a matrix property for current job

### Availability
- Only available for jobs in a matrix configuration
- Changes for each job in a workflow run
- Properties are user-defined in the workflow file
- Accessible from any step in a workflow

### Standard Matrix Properties
No standard properties exist. All properties are defined in the workflow matrix configuration.

### Example Context Contents
For a matrix with `os` and `node` properties:

```json
{
  "os": "ubuntu-latest",
  "node": 16
}
```

This represents a job running with Ubuntu latest OS and Node.js version 16.

### Example Workflow Usage

```yaml
name: Test strategy
on: push

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        test-group: [1, 2]
        node: [14, 16]
    steps:
      - run: echo "Node version: ${{ matrix.node }}"
```

### Usage Notes
- Access matrix values via `${{ matrix.property_name }}`
- Each combination of matrix values creates a separate job
- Useful for testing across multiple environments, versions, or configurations
- Matrix properties must be defined in the workflow file
```

--------------------------------

### Use Matrix Strategy with Reusable Workflow

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows

Shows how to combine a matrix strategy with a reusable workflow to dynamically create multiple job runs based on variable combinations. The matrix context variables are passed as inputs to the reusable workflow, enabling parallel execution with different parameter values.

```yaml
jobs:
  ReusableMatrixJobForDeployment:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        target: [dev, stage, prod]
    uses: octocat/octo-repo/.github/workflows/deployment.yml@main
    with:
      target: ${{ matrix.target }}
```

--------------------------------

### Handle Job Failures with Matrix Strategy in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations

Configure failure handling using `fail-fast` and `continue-on-error` properties. The `fail-fast` property cancels all queued/in-progress jobs when a required job fails, while `continue-on-error` allows individual jobs to fail without affecting others. This example runs four jobs where experimental jobs can fail without cancelling the workflow.

```yaml
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    continue-on-error: ${{ matrix.experimental }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: true
      matrix:
        version: [6, 7, 8]
        experimental: [false]
        include:
          - version: 9
            experimental: true
```

--------------------------------

### Define a Single-Dimension Matrix Strategy in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-your-workflow-with-github-actions/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This YAML snippet illustrates how to define a matrix strategy for a job in GitHub Actions. It uses a single variable `version` to create multiple job runs, each with a different Node.js version. Matrix variables are accessible via the `matrix` context and are used here to configure the `setup-node` action.

```YAML
jobs:
  example_matrix:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        version: [10, 12, 14]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.version }}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Workflow Matrix for Parallel Execution in GitHub Actions

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/contexts

This YAML workflow demonstrates how to use the `matrix` strategy in GitHub Actions to run jobs across different environments. It defines `os` and `node` variables, allowing jobs to execute with various operating systems and Node.js versions, referencing them via `matrix.os` and `matrix.node` respectively.

```yaml
name: Test matrix
on: push

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        node: [14, 16]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node }}
      - name: Output node version
        run: node --version
```

--------------------------------

### Allow Specific GitHub Actions Matrix Jobs to Fail Without Workflow Failure

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/automating-your-workflow-with-github-actions/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions

This example shows how to prevent a workflow run from failing when a specific job within a matrix strategy fails. By setting `continue-on-error: true` for an experimental job (e.g., `node: 15`) and `fail-fast: false` for the overall strategy, only that specific job can fail without causing the entire workflow run to fail.

```yaml
runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
continue-on-error: ${{ matrix.experimental }}
strategy:
  fail-fast: false
  matrix:
    node: [13, 14]
    os: [macos-latest, ubuntu-latest]
    experimental: [false]
    include:
      - node: 15
        os: ubuntu-latest
        experimental: true

```

--------------------------------

### Example JSON Structure of GitHub Actions `matrix` Context

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/contexts

This JSON object provides an example of the `matrix` context within a GitHub Actions workflow. It reflects the specific matrix properties defined in the workflow file that apply to the current job. In this instance, it shows the `os` and `node` values for a job executing with `ubuntu-latest` and Node.js version `16`.

```json
{
  "os": "ubuntu-latest",
  "node": 16
}
```

--------------------------------

### Persist Data Between GitHub Actions Jobs Using Artifacts

Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/migrate-to-github-actions/manual-migrations/migrate-from-circleci

This snippet demonstrates how to save and retrieve data between jobs in a GitHub Actions workflow. It uses the "actions/upload-artifact" action to store a file ("math-homework.txt") after one job completes and the "actions/download-artifact" action to retrieve it in a subsequent job, ensuring data persistence across the workflow. This is crucial for passing build outputs or test results between independent stages.

```yaml
- name: Upload math result for job 1
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: homework
    path: math-homework.txt

...

- name: Download math result for job 1
  uses: actions/download-artifact@v5
  with:
    name: homework
```
